diagrams.title: Image Degradation-Restoration Process Model
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing how an original image f(x,y) is degraded by noise n(x,y) to produce g(x,y), and the restoration process attempts to recover f(x,y)
elements: Original Image f(x,y) - input block (rectangle)
Degradation Operator H - arrow pointing from f to g
Noise n(x,y) - additive block with '+' symbol
Degraded Image g(x,y) - output rectangle
Restoration Filter - arrow from g to restored f̂(x,y)
Restored Image f̂(x,y) - final output block
Arrows show the flow: f(x,y) → H → + n(x,y) → g(x,y) → Restoration → f̂(x,y)
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the forward degradation model and inverse restoration process, which is fundamental to all restoration techniques

diagrams.title: Noise Types Classification
diagrams.description: Hierarchical diagram showing classification of noise types encountered in image processing
elements: Noise Types (central node)
Gaussian Noise - branch with normal distribution curve icon
Salt & Pepper Noise - branch with black and white speckle pattern
Speckle Noise - branch with multiplicative noise formula
Poisson Noise - branch with Poisson distribution formula
Each type shows probability density function shape
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to identify different noise types and understand their statistical properties, which helps in selecting appropriate restoration filters

diagrams.title: Spatial Domain Noise Removal Filters
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing various spatial domain filtering approaches for noise removal
elements: Noisy Image Input - start node
Arithmetic Mean Filter - box with formula
Geometric Mean Filter - box with formula
Harmonic Mean Filter - box with formula
Median Filter - box (for salt & pepper)
Max/Min Filters - boxes for order-statistic filters
Output: Restored Image - end node
Arrows show decision flow between filters based on noise type
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the classification and formulas of spatial domain noise removal filters and when to apply each

diagrams.title: Wiener Filter Process
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing the Wiener filter implementation for image restoration
elements: Input: Degraded Image g(x,y)
FFT Block - converts to frequency domain
Power Spectrum Estimation - computes Ps(f) and Pn(f)
Wiener Filter H(u,v) formula block
Frequency domain multiplication G(u,v) × H(u,v)
Inverse FFT Block - converts back to spatial domain
Output: Restored Image f̂(x,y)
Side branch showing noise variance and image power spectrum estimation
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the frequency domain approach to restoration and how Wiener filter optimally estimates the original image using noise and signal power spectra

diagrams.title: Mean Filter Comparison
diagrams.description: Visual comparison of different mean filter kernels and their effects on noise reduction vs blurring
elements: 3x3 Arithmetic Mean kernel - all weights = 1/9
3x3 Geometric Mean kernel - product form
4x4 Geometric Mean kernel - larger window
Cross-shaped kernel - only center row and column
Weight matrix diagrams showing weight distribution
Comparison arrows showing: more blur → better noise reduction
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize how different kernel sizes and filter types affect the trade-off between noise reduction and image blurring

charts.title: Noise Types and Characteristics
charts.content: Table comparing: Gaussian (continuous, additive, mean=0), Salt & Pepper (impulse, random black/white pixels), Speckle (multiplicative, multiplicative model), Poisson (photon counting, signal-dependent). Columns: Noise Type, Probability Distribution, Additive/Multiplicative, Common Sources, Appearance

charts.title: Spatial Domain Restoration Filters
charts.content: Table comparing: Arithmetic Mean (smooths but blurs), Geometric Mean (better preserves edges), Harmonic Mean (good for salt pepper), Contraharmonic Mean (Q parameter control), Median (excellent for impulse), Max (dark noise), Min (bright noise). Columns: Filter Type, Formula, Best For, Limitations

charts.title: Noise-Restoration Filter Selection Guide
charts.content: Decision tree: Identify noise type → Gaussian: use mean or Wiener filter? → Impulse (salt pepper): use median filter? → Speckle: use geometric mean or adaptive filter? → Output recommended filter

charts.title: Wiener Filter Parameters
charts.content: Table showing: Noise variance (σ²), Image variance (σ²f), Noise-to-Signal ratio (NSR), Filter response behavior. When NSR is large, filter approaches inverse; when NSR is small, filter approaches pseudo-inverse; includes K parameter explanation

mindMaps.centralConcept: Image Restoration in Presence of Noise Only
branches: Noise Types: Gaussian, Salt & Pepper, Speckle, Poisson
Spatial Domain Methods: Mean Filters (Arithmetic, Geometric, Harmonic), Order Statistics (Median, Max, Min)
Frequency Domain Methods: Bandpass filtering, Notch filtering, Wiener filtering
Selection Criteria: Noise type, Image content preservation, Computational complexity
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting noise models, restoration approaches, and selection criteria for exam preparation

branches: Arithmetic Mean: Simple averaging, best noise reduction but most blur
Geometric Mean: Better edge preservation than arithmetic, slightly less smoothing
Harmonic Mean: Excellent for salt pepper noise, fails for gaussian
Contraharmonic Mean: Q parameter allows adaptation, Q>0 for pepper, Q<0 for salt
mindMaps.purpose: Helps students understand relationships between different mean filter variants and their specific use cases
